The reason: context windows are too small for entire websites, and HTML with navigation and ads is hard for machines to parse cleanly. The standard additionally recommends Markdown versions of individual pages at the same URL with .md appended.

## A common misunderstanding
AI-generated comments are not an end in themselves. A comment that only repeats what the line of code already says is noise. Good documentation explains the why – and that is exactly where you as a human need to refine, because the why is often not in the code.
example: Prompt: "Find all functions without a JSDoc comment in src/auth/, generate comments in the style of the existing ones, and flag every spot where you are unsure about the behavior."

quiz.question: What is an llms.txt file for?
options: It stores API keys for AI services
It replaces the README for human readers
It blocks AI crawlers entirely
It gives AI systems a Markdown overview of a website so they can find content without HTML clutter
quiz.explain: llms.txt is a Markdown file at the website root with a title, short description, and link lists – a curated entry page for AI readers, not an access control and not a README replacement.

sources.title: Claude Code Docs: Common Workflows (Handle documentation)

title: Batch Processing: Automating Many Similar Tasks
teaser: Hundreds of similar tasks run automatically: via claude -p in a loop, or through batch APIs at half price (as of July 2026).
body:
## The idea
Some tasks are really many identical tasks: migrate 500 files, classify 2,000 texts, translate every product description. You don't need to chat 500 times for that – batch processing handles them automatically.

## Path 1: headless fan-out
Claude Code also runs without an interactive session via `claude -p "task"`. Inside a script loop over a file list, this becomes a fan-out: one invocation per file. The official docs recommend three steps: generate a task list, build the loop, test on 2–3 files first and sharpen the prompt – then run it on everything. `--allowedTools` limits what the agent may do unattended.

## Path 2: provider batch APIs
For pure model requests without tools there are batch APIs: you submit many requests together and they are processed asynchronously. Anthropic (Message Batches API) and OpenAI (Batch API) charge 50% of the standard prices for this (as of July 2026). At Anthropic, most batches finish in under an hour; at OpenAI a 24-hour window applies (as of July 2026).

## Batch or interactive?
Batch pays off when the tasks are independent and uniform and the result is not needed immediately. Stay interactive when you want to steer, review, or correct along the way – or when the task only becomes clear while working.
bodyDetail:
## Fan-out in detail
The example loop from the Claude Code docs:

```bash
for file in $(cat files.txt); do
  claude -p "Migrate $file from React to Vue. Return OK or FAIL." \
    --allowedTools "Edit,Bash(git commit *)"
done
```

The "Return OK or FAIL" makes the results machine-checkable. For scripts, the docs additionally recommend `--bare`: Claude then starts without local hooks, plugins, and CLAUDE.md, so the run behaves the same on every machine. With `--output-format json` you also get the cost per invocation (total_cost_usd).

## Batch APIs in detail
The flow is the same at both providers: submit the batch, let it process asynchronously, poll the status, collect the results. Key figures (as of July 2026):
- Anthropic Message Batches API: 50% discount on standard prices; most batches finish in under 1 hour; the discount stacks with prompt caching.
- OpenAI Batch API: 50% discount; completion within 24 hours (often faster); up to 50,000 requests or 200 MB per batch; a separate rate-limit pool that does not consume your normal limits.

## Fan-out vs. batch API – which when?
Fan-out starts real agent runs: every invocation can read files, edit them, and execute commands. You need that for code migrations. Batch APIs process pure model requests (text in, text out) without tools – ideal for classification, translation, and analyzing large datasets, and considerably cheaper thanks to the discount.

## A common misunderstanding
Batch does not mean faster. Single requests respond immediately; with batches you wait. The gain is in cost and throughput, not latency. If a human is waiting live for the answer, batch is the wrong path.
example: A team needs to classify 1,200 support tickets by topic. Instead of 1,200 individual requests, it submits one batch to the Message Batches API – half price (as of July 2026), results collected together after the run.

quiz.question: When is a batch API the right choice instead of individual interactive requests?
options: When the answer must reach the user as fast as possible
When the agent is supposed to edit files in the project
When many independent, similar requests are pending and the result is not needed immediately
When only a single complex question needs answering
quiz.explain: Batch APIs process many requests asynchronously at half price (as of July 2026) – in exchange, you wait for the results. For file edits in a project, headless fan-out with claude -p is the right tool.

sources.title: Anthropic Docs: Batch Processing (Message Batches API)
Claude Code Docs: Best Practices (Fan out across files)

title: Managing API Keys Securely
teaser: API keys belong in environment variables or secret managers – never in code, repos, or prompts. If one leaks, only one thing matters: revoke immediately.
body:
## Why this matters
An API key is like a credit card: whoever has it can send requests on your bill. Public repos are automatically scanned for such keys – by attackers and by protection systems alike.

## Rule 1: never in code, repo, or prompt
Keys do not belong in source code, in the git repo, or in chat prompts. The standard local approach: a .env file holding the key – plus a .gitignore entry so it is never committed. Anthropic explicitly recommends exactly this pattern.

## Rule 2: environment variables and secret managers
Locally, your program reads the key from an environment variable. In production, Anthropic recommends encrypted secret storage (the cloud providers' secret managers) instead of dotenv files.

## Rule 3: rotate and restrict
Rotate keys on a regular schedule (Anthropic gives every 90 days as an example), use separate keys for development, testing, and production, and give each key only the minimum rights it needs – the least-privilege principle from the OWASP guide.

## If a key leaks
Revoke and replace immediately. Deleting the commit is not enough: GitHub makes clear that a leaked secret must first be revoked or rotated – the git history may long since have been cloned or cached.
bodyDetail:
## The .env pattern, complete
1. A .env file in the project: `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-...`
2. An entry in .gitignore: `.env`
3. Additionally commit a .env.example without real values, so others can see which variables are needed.
4. In code, read the key only from the environment variable – never store it as a string.

## Why "deleting from git" is not enough
The GitHub docs are explicit: once a secret has leaked, the first step must be to revoke or rotate it ("as a first step you need to revoke and/or rotate that secret"). The reason: clones, forks, caches, and CI logs may already contain the old state. Rotate first, then clean the history if needed.

## Automatic guards
- GitHub push protection blocks pushes containing detected secrets; secret scanning searches public repos.
- Anthropic partners with GitHub: Claude API keys found in public repos are deactivated automatically.
- Pre-commit tools like gitleaks or git-secrets check before every commit – the GitHub docs name both.

## Least privilege per OWASP
The OWASP Secrets Management guide: secrets should carry only the minimum privileges needed, and not every person on the team needs access to all secrets. Separate keys per environment additionally limit the damage: a leaked test key does not endanger production.

## The leak protocol (per OWASP)
1. Revoke: immediately revoke the affected key.
2. Rotate: create a new key and roll it out everywhere.
3. Delete: remove the leaked key from the exposed system.
Afterwards: check logs and usage data for what happened with the key.

## Agent-specific note
Never paste keys into prompts – prompts end up in histories and logs. An agent that reads a .env has the key in its context afterwards; grant that access only when truly necessary.
example: Instead of `apiKey = "sk-ant-..."` in code: put the key in .env (.env is listed in .gitignore), read `process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in code – and use your cloud provider's secret manager in production.

quiz.question: Your API key accidentally ended up in a public repo. What is the correct first step?
options: Delete the commit and force-push – then the key is gone
Wait and see whether the key is actually abused
Revoke or rotate the key immediately, and only then clean up the history
Set the repo to private
quiz.explain: The git history may long since have been cloned or cached. GitHub explicitly recommends revoking or rotating the secret first – deleting alone does not make the key unusable.

sources.title: Anthropic Help Center: API Key Best Practices
GitHub Docs: Removing sensitive data from a repository

exercise.task: Set up the .env pattern in a practice project and verify that no key can end up in the repo.
steps: Create a .env with a dummy variable (NO real key) and add .env to .gitignore.
Create a .env.example with the variable names but no values, and commit only that.
Check with `git status` that .env does not appear as a new file – and read the value in code via the environment variable.
selfCheck: Does .env show up in `git status`? (If yes: check the .gitignore entry)
Is there a key as a plaintext string anywhere in your code or in a prompt?
Do you know where you could revoke your real key immediately (the provider's console/dashboard)?

title: Running AI Agents in Large Monorepos
teaser: In a monorepo, an AI agent easily loses focus and drifts into the wrong subproject — the right CLAUDE.md structure keeps it on track.
body:
## The problem with big repos
A monorepo often holds many subprojects: a backend, a frontend, shared libraries. One single CLAUDE.md for everything either grows huge (eating up context on every session, even for tasks that don't need it) or stays so generic it barely helps.

## The fix: multiple CLAUDE.md files
Instead of one file for everything, give each subproject its own CLAUDE.md. A file at the repo root holds rules that apply everywhere (commit style, overall layout). Each subproject gets its own file with its specific commands and conventions.

## How loading actually works
Start the agent at the repo root, and it loads only the root CLAUDE.md at launch; files in subfolders load only once the agent actually reads files there. Start it directly inside a subproject, and it gets that folder's file plus every parent one — but by default only file access to that subtree.

## Forcing focus
For tasks that clearly belong to one subproject, it pays to start the agent right there instead of at the root. That keeps context and file access narrow automatically, without you having to explain on every task which parts of the repo are irrelevant right now.
bodyDetail:
## The load order in detail
At startup, Claude Code walks the directory you launched it from plus every parent directory looking for CLAUDE.md files, and loads them fully into context — from the root down to your starting directory, so instructions closest to where you launched are read last. Files in subdirectories below your starting point load differently: only once the agent actually reads a file in that subfolder does its CLAUDE.md load too.

## claudeMdExcludes against other teams' files
In a very large monorepo, working from the repo root can pull in other teams' CLAUDE.md files the moment the agent happens to read a file in their folder. The `claudeMdExcludes` setting lets you skip specific paths or patterns — legacy packages, or other teams' folders that are irrelevant to the current task.

## Where you start determines access
Where you launch the agent decides not just which CLAUDE.md files load automatically, but also which files it can touch without asking. From the repo root it sees the whole repo; from a subfolder, at first only that subtree. If a task needs access to a sibling package — say, a shared type used across several packages — you can grant that explicitly with `additionalDirectories` or the `--add-dir` flag instead of opening everything up front.

## Per-directory skills
Skills can live per subproject too (`.claude/skills/` inside each folder). A skill with API testing patterns loads only when the agent is actually working in the API package — it stays out of the way during frontend work. That way, available context doesn't automatically grow with the size of the repo.

## A common misconception
More CLAUDE.md files doesn't automatically mean more context load. The opposite is true: layered correctly, a collection of small, targeted files replaces one giant file that's always loaded in full. The goal isn't "as many rules as possible" — it's that every task only loads the rules that actually apply to the part being worked on.
example: A monorepo with packages/api, packages/web, and packages/shared gets a root CLAUDE.md ('Three packages: api (Node/Express), web (React/Vite), shared (TS utils). Always run commands from the relevant package folder.') plus its own packages/api/CLAUDE.md with API-specific test commands. Start the agent directly inside packages/api/, and it sees both files — but nothing from packages/web/.

quiz.question: By default, what happens when you start Claude Code directly inside a monorepo subfolder (e.g. packages/api/) instead of the repo root?
options: It automatically loads every other package's CLAUDE.md too, such as packages/web/
It loads that subfolder's CLAUDE.md plus every parent folder's, and initially has file access only to that subtree
It ignores every CLAUDE.md and relies only on the system prompt
It automatically gets access to the entire repository, including every sibling package
quiz.explain: Starting from a subfolder, Claude Code loads that folder's CLAUDE.md plus every parent directory's, but by default only has file access to that subtree — access to sibling packages must be granted explicitly via additionalDirectories or --add-dir.

sources.title: Claude Code docs: Monorepos and large codebases
Claude Code docs: CLAUDE.md and memory

title: Safe Refactoring with AI Agents
teaser: An agent can quietly change behavior instead of just structure during a refactor — tests and small steps prevent that.
body:
## What refactoring actually means
Refactoring means improving code's internal structure without changing its externally visible behavior. The term was popularized by Martin Fowler's book of the same name. The "without changing behavior" part is the crucial bit — and it's exactly what gets lost with AI agents if nobody insists on it.

## Why agents slip up here
While restructuring, an agent often spots things that look "wrong" to it — an odd condition, a duplicate check. Without a clear instruction, it fixes that along the way instead of just restructuring. A pure refactor quietly turns into a behavior change.

## The three key safeguards
1. Tests first: before restructuring, a test suite must exist that locks in current behavior. Without tests you can't verify whether anything changed.
2. Small steps: one change per step, run tests after each step, commit each green step separately.
3. Demand behavior preservation explicitly: say "refactor without changing behavior" instead of just "make this code better."

## Typical traps
The agent silently changes a public interface, deletes code that looks dead to it but is actually called dynamically, or mixes refactoring with feature requests in one pass.
bodyDetail:
## The difference between refactoring and rewriting
A true refactor is behavior-preserving by definition: at the end, the code returns the same outputs for the same inputs — only the structure differs. An agent that corrects an edge case, changes an error message, or removes a check it judges redundant during a "refactor" has left the assignment, even if the change looks objectively reasonable. Changes like that belong in their own, separately reviewed step, not the same refactor.

## Why small steps make the difference
A single giant refactoring request ("clean up the whole module") is nearly impossible to verify: too many changes happen at once, and one failing test doesn't tell you which of twenty changes caused it. Ask instead for one step at a time — extract a function, test, commit; extract the next function, test, commit — and every failure stays locally traceable. Claude Code's own guidance for complex tasks lines up with this: plan first (plan mode), implement incrementally, test after each file, and interrupt immediately at the first sign of a wrong direction instead of letting it run.

## No tests, no safe refactor
For code with zero test coverage, behavior preservation can't be verified mechanically at all — not by you, not by the agent. In that case the first step isn't the refactor itself, but writing tests that lock in the current (not the desired) behavior before anything gets restructured.

## A common misconception
"The AI got the tests green" doesn't automatically prove behavior preservation — it only proves the existing tests didn't measure anything else. Test coverage is always a lower bound on your safety net, never a guarantee. For critical code, a manual diff review by a human is worth the extra step, especially in places tests don't cover.
example: Prompt: 'Extract the validation logic from processOrder() into its own function, validateOrder(). Behavior must not change. After extracting, run the existing test suite and stop to report if any test fails before continuing.'

quiz.question: What's the single most important prerequisite before letting an AI agent do a significant refactor on existing code?
options: A test suite (or tests written first) that locks in current behavior
As long and detailed a prompt as possible, spelling out every single step
A particularly powerful model — that alone is enough
A cleaned-up git history with old commits removed
quiz.explain: Without tests, there's no way to verify that a refactor truly left behavior unchanged. Tests are the safeguard that exposes the difference between a genuine refactor and an unnoticed behavior change.

exercise.task: Take a function from one of your own projects that does too much at once, and have an agent refactor it in small, test-backed steps.
steps: Check first whether a test covers the function. If not, have a test written first that locks in current behavior.
Ask the agent explicitly: 'Extract [part X] into its own function, behavior must not change. Run tests after each step.'
Read the diff before committing — look specifically for changes that go beyond pure restructuring.
selfCheck: Are all tests still green after the refactor — and had that already been checked beforehand?
Does the diff contain any line that doesn't just move code, but changes a condition, a value, or an error message?
Was everything committed in one shot, or could individual steps be traced separately?

title: Understanding and Modernizing Old Codebases with AI
teaser: Legacy code without tests can't just be rewritten — characterization tests and small steps make AI-assisted modernization safe.
body:
## What makes legacy code different
Software developer Michael Feathers once defined legacy code bluntly as "code without tests." That's exactly what makes it dangerous to change: nobody — not even an AI agent — can verify whether a change breaks behavior if there are no tests capturing that behavior in the first place.

## Step one: capture behavior, don't improve it
Before restructuring anything, you need characterization tests. These are tests that write down what the code does RIGHT NOW — even if that looks odd or outright buggy. They're not a quality judgment, just a snapshot that protects later changes.

## Step two: migrate incrementally
A full rewrite is risky: nothing runs reliably until the new version is done, and subtle old behavior often gets lost along the way. Incremental migration is the more proven approach — a pattern known as the "strangler fig": new pieces grow around the old code bit by bit, until the old one can eventually be switched off.

## Where AI agents help most
An agent can quickly summarize large, undocumented modules and propose candidate characterization tests — work that's normally very costly without documentation. What still matters: understand and secure first, only then change.
bodyDetail:
## The principle behind characterization tests
A characterization test differs from a normal unit test in intent: a normal test checks whether code does what it SHOULD do. A characterization test only checks whether code does what it does RIGHT NOW — regardless of whether that's correct. In practice, you run the existing code with typical inputs, write the actual output down as the expected value in the test, and end up with a baseline any later change can be checked against.

## Seams: where you can even get a foothold
Legacy code is often tightly tangled — a function depends on a global variable, a constructor opens a database connection directly. Code like that is nearly impossible to test in isolation. The term "seam," coined by Feathers, describes a place in the code where you can alter behavior without editing the code at that exact spot — for example, through dependency injection at an interface. An agent can help find such seams in an unfamiliar codebase in the first place.

## The strangler fig pattern, concretely
In incremental migration, the new implementation runs alongside the old one, initially handling only a small slice of cases (say, one route, one customer type). A router decides which requests go to the old version and which to the new one. As confidence in the new version grows, more traffic shifts there, until the old implementation eventually disappears entirely. The advantage: the system stays operational throughout the migration, and a failure only affects the slice currently being migrated.

## A typical trap in AI-assisted modernization
An agent that stumbles onto a strange-looking rule while understanding old code ("why does this subtract a discount instead of adding it for orders under 5 euros?") is happy to fix it "in passing." Without an explicit instruction to modernize only, and not touch business logic, rules like this — which may have grown that way on purpose — quietly disappear, and nobody notices until a customer complains.
example: Prompt: 'This 1,800-line module has no tests and no docs. First summarize what it does functionally. Then write characterization tests for the five most important functions that capture the CURRENT behavior — even if it looks odd. Don't change any code yet.'

quiz.question: What is a characterization test?
options: A test that captures the current behavior of untested code, regardless of whether that behavior is correct
A test that only measures a system's performance
A test that gets generated automatically the moment an agent reads code
A test that checks whether new code meets the official requirements
quiz.explain: Characterization tests capture what code does RIGHT NOW, not what it should do. They create a baseline for untested code that later changes can be checked against.

exercise.task: Take an untested, tangled module from one of your own (or an open-source) projects and lock it down with characterization tests before changing anything.
steps: Have the agent first summarize what the module does functionally, without changing any code.
Together, identify 3-5 typical inputs/calls and have the agent write tests that capture the current outputs as expected values.
Only then: make one small, isolated change and check whether the characterization tests are still green.
selfCheck: Do the tests cover the most important paths, not just the simplest case?
Did you deliberately capture the CURRENT behavior instead of already 'improving' it while testing?
Is everything still green after the first change — or does a failing test show exactly what changed?

title: How AI Agents Remember Things
teaser: An agent starts every session with a blank mind — memory systems like CLAUDE.md and memory files bridge that gap.
body:
## The core problem
Every new session of an AI agent begins with an empty context window. Without extra mechanisms, the agent has no idea what happened yesterday — which rules apply, what went wrong last time, which library your team uses.

## Two kinds of memory
You can roughly split memory into two kinds. Semantic memory is fixed, durable rules and facts — "we use pnpm, not npm," code style, architecture decisions. That lives in files like CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md, which you maintain. Episodic memory is notes about specific past events — "in session X, approach Y failed because Z." Notes like that are often written by the agent itself, in separate memory files.

## How this looks technically
CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md loads in full at every session. Memory files often work differently: a short index file always loads, while more detailed topic files load only on demand — much like a table of contents pointing to detail pages.

## Compaction
When context inside a running session gets too full, the agent automatically summarizes older parts of the conversation to free up room (compaction). That's a different mechanism from persistent files: it works within a session, not across sessions.
bodyDetail:
## Semantic vs. episodic memory in detail
The distinction originally comes from cognitive psychology and maps cleanly onto agents. Semantic knowledge is stable and general — it changes rarely and holds regardless of when it was written down. Episodic knowledge is tied to a specific event — it has a timestamp, a context, often an outcome ("worked" / "failed"). In Claude Code, CLAUDE.md corresponds to the semantic side (human-written, stable, loaded in full every session), while auto-memory files cover the episodic side: notes the agent writes itself from corrections and observations made while working.

## The index file as a compromise
A purely episodic collection would grow huge over time and eventually blow the context budget itself. The fix is a two-tier model: a short overview file (often just the first few hundred lines) always loads automatically and points to more detailed topic files, which get read only when actually needed. That keeps the base context small while detail knowledge stays available when it matters.

## Compaction as a third element
Beyond the two written memory forms, there's a third mechanism that works within a single, long session: compaction. As context approaches its limit, the model summarizes older messages, deliberately keeping key decisions, open bugs, and implementation details, while dropping redundant intermediate steps. The art lies in not summarizing too aggressively and losing information that's important but easy to overlook.

## A common misconception
A memory or CLAUDE.md file doesn't "learn" on its own in any human sense. Every line had to be written once — either by you, or by the agent based on a concrete observation made while working. Left unmaintained, a file grows inaccurate over time, just like stale documentation. Persistent memory doesn't replace upkeep — it just shifts where the information lives.
example: An example split: CLAUDE.md says 'Tests run with npm test, never use npm run test:ci locally' (semantic, durable). A memory file says 'Session from July 12: migration with db:push failed on a foreign-key violation — try db:migrate:dry-run first next time' (episodic, tied to a specific event).

quiz.question: What best distinguishes an episodic memory note from a rule in CLAUDE.md?
options: The memory note is tied to a specific past event, while the CLAUDE.md rule holds permanently and generally
Memory notes never load automatically, while CLAUDE.md loads on every single tool call
There's no real difference — both terms mean exactly the same thing
CLAUDE.md can only be written by the AI itself, memory notes only by humans
quiz.explain: Episodic notes capture specific, time-bound events ('in session X, Y failed'), while CLAUDE.md holds durable, general-purpose rules that apply regardless of any single event.

sources.title: Claude Code docs: CLAUDE.md and auto memory
Anthropic: Effective context engineering for AI agents

title: Cutting Agent Costs: A Practical Checklist
teaser: Four levers meaningfully cut AI agent costs: the right model, caching, a lean context, and batch processing.
body:
## Why costs spiral fast
An agent doesn't call the model once — it often calls it dozens of times per task, for planning, tool use, intermediate steps. Without deliberate control, you frequently pay top price for work that never needed it.

## Lever 1: model routing
Not every subtask needs the strongest model. Mechanical work — classifying, formatting, simple summaries — goes to a cheap, fast model. Complex planning and hard decisions stay with the strong model.

## Lever 2: caching
When an agent repeatedly sends the same long prefix (system prompt, tool definitions), prompt caching means that part doesn't have to be recomputed every time — as long as requests follow each other within a short time window.

## Lever 3: keep context lean
Disable tools you're not using, clear context between unrelated tasks, pre-filter verbose log or test output instead of passing it through whole, and offload detail knowledge into skills instead of dragging it along in CLAUDE.md permanently.

## Lever 4: batch processing
For large volumes of non-urgent requests (bulk classification, evaluations), a batch API beats individual calls — significantly cheaper, in exchange for no immediate response.

These four levers combine: the cheapest model that still does the job, with a cached prompt prefix, a lean context, processed in batch.
bodyDetail:
## Model routing, with numbers
Anthropic's own pricing (as of July 2026) shows the scale involved: a cheap model like Claude Haiku costs a fraction per million tokens compared to the most capable model. For subagents with a clearly mechanical subtask, many agent frameworks let you pin a cheaper model directly instead of automatically inheriting the main task's model.

## Caching, with numbers (as of July 2026)
A cache hit with Claude costs only a fraction of the normal input-token price, while a cache write costs somewhat more than normal. The default cache lives for 5 minutes; anyone sending many requests in quick succession (say, an agent loop with lots of intermediate steps) benefits most, because the expensive prefix gets written once but read cheaply many times.

## Keeping context lean — concrete levers
Anthropic's own guidance for Claude Code lists several very specific techniques: clear context between unrelated tasks instead of letting it grow, keep CLAUDE.md compact (rule of thumb: under 200 lines, with detail knowledge moved into skills loaded on demand instead), and automatically filter verbose raw output — a 10,000-line log file, say — before it reaches the model, so only the relevant lines (errors, for instance) land in context instead of the whole file.

## Batch processing, with numbers (as of July 2026)
The Message Batches API cuts costs by 50% compared to individual calls, and most batches finish in under an hour. The trade-off: no immediate, synchronous response — in exchange for a much lower price on large, non-time-critical volumes like bulk evaluations or test runs across thousands of cases.

## A warning: parallel agents
Multiple agent instances running in parallel (an agent team, say) multiply costs, because each instance maintains its own context window — in unfavorable cases this can push token usage several times higher than a single session. Parallelism saves time, rarely money; the benefit has to justify that.
example: A nightly job classifies 8,000 support tickets by urgency. Instead of querying each ticket individually and synchronously with the strongest model: classification rules and examples form a shared, cached prompt prefix, a cheap model handles the actual classification, and all 8,000 requests run together through the batch API instead of one by one.

quiz.question: Which combination typically cuts the cost of a nightly bulk classification job the most?
options: Using only the most expensive available model, for maximum speed
Spinning up as many parallel agent instances as possible
Handling every request individually and synchronously, rebuilding context from scratch each time
A cheap model for the mechanical classification, a cached shared prompt prefix, and processing via the batch API
quiz.explain: A mechanical classification job typically benefits most from combining a cheap model, a cached and reused prompt prefix, and the cheaper, asynchronous batch API — not from an expensive model, a fully rebuilt context per request, or unnecessary parallelism.

sources.title: Claude Code docs: Manage costs effectively

exercise.task: Analyze one of your own recurring agent workflows against the four cost levers and identify where at least one of them goes unused.
steps: List your workflow's steps and mark which model currently handles which step.
Check: is there a long, repeated prompt prefix (system prompt, rules, examples) that would benefit from caching?
Check: does the workflow run time-critically/synchronously, or could part of it run as a non-urgent batch instead?
selfCheck: Is at least one mechanical substep currently running unnecessarily on an expensive model?
Do you know whether your prompt prefix gets reused within the cache TTL, or gets rewritten every time?
Is there a part of your workflow that doesn't need an immediate answer and would fit batch processing?

title: Security Audits with AI: Checking Code for Vulnerabilities
teaser: AI agents catch a lot of security issues automatically – but critical findings always need a human to confirm them.
body:
## What an AI security audit does
An AI agent reads your code or a diff and scans it for known vulnerability patterns: SQL injection, unsafe deserialization, hardcoded passwords, missing authorization checks. That's far faster than reading every line by hand.

## Typical findings
Common finds include injection flaws (SQL, command, XSS), missing authorization checks, exposed secrets like API keys, weak cryptography, and unsafe calls like `eval()`. These patterns show up in real code again and again, usually from time pressure or oversight.

## Where the limits are
An agent can spot patterns, but it doesn't automatically understand your threat model: who the attacker is, what the most valuable target is, which trade-offs are acceptable. A finding can be harmless in one context and critical in another – only someone who knows the system can tell the difference.

## A human checks anything critical
Automated review is a first pass, not a replacement for human judgment. For findings touching authentication, payment data, or access rights especially: get a human to confirm before you mark it fixed or dismiss it. And a security tool built on AI can itself be fooled by malicious text hidden in code – reviewing unknown, untrusted code needs extra caution.
bodyDetail:
## Defense layers in practice
A solid setup combines several layers that check at different depths. A simple pattern match with no model call can react instantly to risky calls like `eval()` or `os.system` on every code change, at no extra cost. A second layer has a fresh model instance review the diff for security at the end of each turn; it catches more complex patterns – authorization gaps, insecure direct object references, server-side request forgery – that plain text search misses. A third, deeper layer reviews every commit or push together with callers and surrounding context, to cut down on false positives.

## Human review stays mandatory
None of these layers automatically blocks writing or committing code – findings get reported, not enforced. For pull requests, multi-stage reviews add specialized agents searching in parallel for different bug classes, plus a verification step that checks candidates against actual code behavior to filter out false positives. One well-known GitHub Action for automated PR review covers injection, authentication flaws, exposed secrets, and weak cryptography – but its documentation explicitly warns that the tool is not hardened against prompt injection and should only run on trusted pull requests.

## Threat model first
Before turning an agent loose on your code, it's worth a quick upfront think: who might attack your system, what do they want, and which parts are most valuable (payment data, login, admin rights)? These are exactly the questions that classic security catalogs like the OWASP Top 10 provide a framework for – injection, broken authentication, security misconfiguration, and similar categories. An agent can work through these categories systematically, but weighing what's actually critical in your case stays a matter of interpretation.

## A common misunderstanding
"The agent didn't find anything" doesn't mean "the code is secure." It only means the patterns checked in that pass didn't match. Automated reviews are one of several defense layers, not a substitute for a real security audit on critical infrastructure.
example: Example prompt: 'Use a subagent and check the login route's diff for security issues: injection, missing authorization, exposed secrets. Categorize each finding by OWASP category and flag what a human needs to confirm before merge.'

quiz.question: Why doesn't an AI security review replace human judgment on critical findings?
options: Because AI reviews are fundamentally slower than manual reviews
Because an agent can spot patterns, but doesn't automatically know your threat model or how critical a finding actually is
Because AI models cannot read code
Because automated reviews only work on frontend code
quiz.explain: An agent recognizes known patterns, but whether a finding is truly critical in your specific system depends on context only a human can fully assess.

sources.title: Claude Code Docs: Security Guidance Plugin

exercise.task: Have an agent review a small, deliberately insecure code snippet (e.g. a login function with string-concatenated SQL) for security issues.
steps: Write or grab a function with a known flaw, e.g. `query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='" + input + "'"`.
Have the agent review the function and categorize findings by OWASP category (injection, auth, etc.).
Ask specifically: 'Which of these findings would need human confirmation before a real merge, and why?'
selfCheck: Did the agent correctly identify and name the SQL injection?
Did it assess severity realistically, or just say 'dangerous' in general?
Could you explain why a finding like this needs human confirmation before you mark it resolved?

title: Databases with Agents: Safe, Not Risky
teaser: An agent with database access can break more in seconds than a human could in hours – read-only first protects you from that.
body:
## Why databases are especially risky
An agent that writes bad code produces a bug. An agent that runs a bad database query can delete or corrupt real data – often with no way to undo it. That's why databases need stricter rules than ordinary code.

## Read-only first
For research and debugging, give an agent read access only. An agent that can only run `SELECT` queries can help you understand data and schema without being able to destroy anything. Write access gets added only when truly necessary – and even then, scoped tightly.

## Migration discipline
Schema changes (migrations) never go straight into a live database. They run through versioned migration files that get reviewed, tested, and only then applied. An agent can propose and write a migration – but running it should go through a controlled process, not a spontaneous shell command.

## Never touch prod without a gate
Access to the production database always needs a deliberate gate: a human confirming, a separate access token, or both. An agent should never be able to automatically hop from staging to production.

## Don't skip backups
Before any write operation runs, no matter how small: a current backup must exist. That's the last line of defense when everything else fails.
bodyDetail:
## Locking down permissions technically
In practice, read access is best enforced at the database role level: a DB user with SELECT-only privileges, no rights for INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or DDL commands. That's stronger than any instruction in a prompt, because it doesn't depend on the agent's behavior – the database itself enforces it. On top of that, tools like Claude Code let you set permission rules that deny specific Bash commands or tools outright (`permissions.deny`), plus hooks that can, for example, block any write attempt into a migrations folder before it ever runs.

## Why migrations need their own system
A migration that runs directly and unreviewed against a live database can damage production data instantly if something goes wrong – and unlike code, there's often no simple "git revert" for that. A clean migration workflow runs in stages: test the migration locally or on a copy, have a human review it, apply it through a controlled pipeline rather than manually, and keep a documented rollback path in case something breaks.

## What the prod gate looks like concretely
A gate can take different technical shapes: separate credentials for staging and production, an approval step in the deploy pipeline, or simply the rule that production access never happens in the same agent run as experimental tinkering. What matters is that switching to prod is a deliberate, visible step – not a side effect of an overly permissive tool setup.

## A common misunderstanding
"The agent only tried it once" is no excuse – a single wrong `DELETE` or `DROP` is enough. The MCP project's (now archived) Postgres reference server explicitly advertised read-only access with schema inspection as its default safety measure — and such reference implementations are equally clear that they're meant as references, and production use needs your own security review on top.

## A deeper example
An agent investigating a bug where user orders display incorrectly: with read-only DB access, it can query the affected rows, understand the schema, and form a hypothesis – with zero risk. Only once the cause is clear does it write a migration or fix command, which a human reviews on staging before it ever runs.
example: Example setup: an agent gets access through a DB user called `readonly_agent` that only has SELECT rights on the staging database. For schema changes, the agent writes a migration file (e.g. with a migration tool) that only gets applied through the regular pipeline after human review – never directly via a shell command on prod.

quiz.question: What's the safest baseline rule for an agent's access to a database?
options: Give the agent full write access from the start so it can work faster
Deny agents database access altogether
Read-only access by default, write access only scoped and limited, and production access only through a deliberate gate
Backups are only necessary for very large databases
quiz.explain: Read-only first, tightly scoped write access, and a deliberate gate for production access minimize the risk of an agent's mistake damaging real data.

title: Understanding Unfamiliar Code with AI
teaser: Instead of clicking through unfamiliar code for hours, let an agent explore and summarize it first.
body:
## The old way vs. the new way
You used to work through a new project file by file. With an AI agent, you can instead ask: "Give me an overview of this codebase" – and get in seconds a structure that would otherwise have taken hours.

## Let it explore
An agent can read files, spot patterns, and describe relationships without you looking at every line yourself. Start broad: "How is the project structured?", "What are the key data models?", "How is authentication handled?" – then get more specific.

## Architecture summary
A good first step is an architecture summary: what the main components are, how they interact, which patterns get used. That gives you a map before you get lost in details.

## Ask targeted questions
You can ask exactly the way you'd ask an experienced colleague: "What does this function on line 134 do?", "Why does this call `foo()` instead of `bar()`?", "What edge cases does this code handle?" These questions don't need special phrasing – just ask directly.

## Diagrams help
For complex flows, ask for a diagram or a text sketch of the flow, for example how a request travels from the frontend to the database. That makes invisible connections visible.
bodyDetail:
## Broad before narrow
The most effective sequence is always broad first, narrow later: understand the overall structure first, then individual components, then individual lines. Asking about a detail immediately, without knowing the context, often gets a technically correct but not very useful answer. The official docs recommend exactly this order: ask for a high-level overview first, then dive into individual components, and only then ask about coding conventions or a glossary of project-specific terms.

## Finding code instead of searching for it
To find code related to a specific feature, it helps to first have the agent explicitly identify the files involved, then ask how those files work together, and only then have it trace the full execution path – for example from the login form in the frontend down to the database query. Using the project's own domain language (instead of your own paraphrasing) noticeably improves the hit rate.

## Subagents for large-scale research
In large codebases, every file the agent reads fills up your main conversation's context. For extensive exploration, it's worth delegating the research to a subagent: it reads many files in its own, separate context and reports back only a summary. That keeps your main conversation manageable while the search work happens in the background.

## A common misunderstanding
An agent producing a summary hasn't automatically understood the code correctly – it's pattern-matching against training data and whatever it has read. For security-critical or particularly tangled spots, it's worth asking specifically: "Are you sure? Show me the actual code that proves this." A summary without a line reference is a hint, not proof.

## A deeper example
For a new project with unfamiliar login logic: start with "Give me an overview of the codebase," then "Explain the main architecture patterns," then "How is authentication handled?", and only then "Trace the flow from the login form to session creation, with file references."
example: Prompt sequence for a new project: 1) 'Give me an overview of this codebase.' 2) 'Explain the main architecture patterns.' 3) 'Find the files that handle user authentication, and trace the flow from the login form to the database.'

quiz.question: What's the most effective way to quickly get oriented in an unfamiliar codebase with AI?
options: Immediately ask about the most complicated function in the code
Read every file yourself before even using the AI
Only ask which programming language is used
Start with a broad overview question, then work step by step toward individual components and details
quiz.explain: Asking broad before narrow – overview first, then components, then details – produces far more useful answers than jumping straight to detail questions without context.

sources.title: Claude Code Docs: Common Workflows (Understand new codebases)

title: Frontend with AI: Screenshots as Feedback
teaser: An agent can't see your UI – until you show it a screenshot. Visual feedback is the key to good frontend work with AI.
body:
## The problem: AI can't see your UI
An agent writing code doesn't automatically "see" the result in a browser. It doesn't know whether a button is misaligned or text is overflowing – unless you show it. That's the difference between "make the dashboard nicer" (vague) and a screenshot plus a concrete instruction.

## Screenshots as feedback
You can paste screenshots directly: drag and drop, copy-paste, or as a file path. Give the agent an image of the design you want, then have it take a screenshot of the result and compare. That creates a loop the agent can close itself, instead of you checking every intermediate step manually.

## Iterating on components
Work in small steps: build one component, take a screenshot, name the differences from the target, fix them. That works better than "build the whole page" at once, because mistakes surface early.

## Use your design system
If your project has a design system (fixed colors, spacing, components), tell the agent explicitly or show it existing components as a template. That stops it from inventing its own, inconsistent solutions.

## Verify visually
In the end, what counts is the visible result, not the code. Always ask for a screenshot of the actual result, not just a claim that it's "done."
bodyDetail:
## Why a reference image makes the difference
The official best-practices docs make the contrast explicit: "make the dashboard nicer" produces a random result, because there's no success criterion. A pasted screenshot plus "implement this design, then take a screenshot of the result yourself, compare it to the original, and list the differences" gives the agent a checkable goal instead. The agent can run this loop – implement, screenshot, compare, adjust – on its own, multiple times, without you looking in after every single step.

## The verification loop, technically
For an agent to actually close this loop itself, it needs a tool that can genuinely take a screenshot in a browser – for example, browser automation it can call as a tool. Without such a tool, "verification" stays a claim the agent makes, not a real check. That's exactly the same principle that applies to tests or build checks: an agent that can check its own result against a real signal (pass/fail, or here: a visual comparison) works more reliably than one that just claims to be done.

## Small steps beat big leaps
Frontend work with agents works best component by component: one component per pass, with immediate visual verification, rather than demanding a whole page in one shot. Mistakes in a single component are easier to spot and describe than mistakes spread across an entire page.

## A common misunderstanding
"Looks good to me" from the agent's own description is not proof. An agent can write code that looks syntactically correct but is visually way off – wrong spacing or overlapping elements, for instance, that only an actual screenshot reveals. So always ask for the evidence (a screenshot), not just the claim.

## A deeper example
For a new pricing table: paste the design mockup as an image, prompt "Implement this design using our existing card components from @src/components/Card.tsx. Then take a screenshot and compare it to the mockup, list the deviations, and fix them." The agent then iterates on its own until the mockup and the screenshot match.
example: Prompt with an image: '[target design screenshot attached] Implement this design for the dashboard header. Use our existing components from @src/components. Then take a screenshot of the result, compare it to the original, and list the differences.'

quiz.question: Why is a screenshot of the target design more effective than the prompt 'make the dashboard nicer'?
options: Because screenshots are shorter than text descriptions
Because AI agents process images faster than text
Because a screenshot gives the agent a concrete, checkable goal it can compare against its own screenshot
Because images eliminate the need for a design system
quiz.explain: A concrete target image plus an instruction to self-compare gives the agent a success criterion it can check on its own – a vague instruction produces a random result instead.

sources.title: Claude Code Docs: Best Practices (Give Claude a way to verify its work)
Claude Code Docs: Common Workflows (Work with images)

exercise.task: Take a small UI component from a practice project (or build a simple card component) and have an agent iterate it against a reference image.
steps: Find or create a reference image (a screenshot of a website, a design mockup) and attach it for the agent.
Prompt: 'Implement this design as a component. Then take a screenshot of the result and compare it to the original.'
Let the agent self-correct at least once based on the comparison before you step in yourself.
selfCheck: Did the agent actually take a screenshot and compare, or just claim it looked good?
What concrete deviations did the comparison reveal?
Was the result closer to the original after self-correction than the first attempt?

title: Pair Programming Patterns with AI
teaser: Humans and AI work best together when it's clear who's driving and who's navigating – and when you step in.
body:
## Driver and navigator
Classic pair programming gives us an image that fits working with AI too: one person "drives" (writes/executes), the other "navigates" (thinks along, corrects, keeps the big picture). With an agent, you usually flip that role: the agent drives (writes the code), you navigate (set direction, correct early).

## Plan, review, execute
A proven pattern has three phases: have a plan created first (no code yet), read and correct the plan, only then have it implemented. That stops the agent from writing a lot of code for the wrong solution.

## When to step in
Step in as soon as you notice things heading the wrong way – not only at the end. If you've had to correct the same mistake twice, a restart with a more precise prompt usually beats a third correction.

## Feedback loops
The shortest loop is the best one: the agent takes a step, you (or a second agent) check it, feedback goes back, next step. That applies to code just as much as to reviews – a fresh look from someone who didn't write the solution themselves catches more.
bodyDetail:
## The four phases in detail
A workflow that has proven itself in practice: explore (the agent reads code, asks clarifying questions, changes nothing), plan (the agent proposes a concrete implementation plan, you read and correct it), implement (the agent builds it, following the plan, writing tests), finish (commit with a meaningful message, open a PR). Separating planning from implementation is the single biggest lever: letting an agent code immediately risks a lot of work flowing in the wrong direction before anyone notices. Planning pays off especially when scope is unclear, multiple files are affected, or you don't know the code well yourself – for a one-line change, it's unnecessary overhead.

## Writer/reviewer as a second pattern
A second proven pattern separates writing and evaluating into different sessions: one session implements, a second session with fresh context reviews the diff against clear criteria, the first session addresses the feedback. The reason: whoever wrote something tends to think it's correct. A reviewer without that bias, seeing only the diff and the criteria, evaluates more independently. The same pattern works for tests: one session writes tests, another writes code that passes them.

## Stepping in: early, not late
Tight feedback loops beat rare, big corrections. A short stop as soon as an agent visibly drifts off course saves more time than waiting until the end. As a rule of thumb: if the same mistake has been corrected twice, the context is usually already clogged with failed approaches – restarting with a more precise prompt beats a third correction in the same conversation almost every time.

## A common misunderstanding
Pair programming with AI doesn't mean watching the whole time. It means stepping in at the right points: at the plan, at intermediate stages, at the final review. In between, the agent can work independently – as long as it has a signal it can check its own result against.
example: Workflow for a new feature: 1) 'Explore how we currently handle sessions, and read the relevant files. No code yet.' 2) 'Create a plan for Google OAuth login.' – read the plan, cut a step that's too big. 3) 'Implement the plan, write tests for the callback.' 4) 'Use a subagent and review the diff against the plan – report gaps, not style preferences.'

quiz.question: What's the core benefit of splitting planning and implementation into separate steps when working with an agent?
options: You can correct the approach before a lot of code gets written for the wrong solution
It fundamentally saves tokens because less gets written
Agents cannot write any code at all without an explicit planning step
It removes the need to test the result later
quiz.explain: A plan before code lets you catch mistakes early – before the agent has invested a lot of work into the wrong approach.

sources.title: Claude Code Docs: Best Practices (Explore, plan, code, commit)

exercise.task: Practice the plan-review-execute workflow on a small real task in your project.
steps: Pick a small but non-trivial task (e.g. a new form field with validation).
Have only a plan created first, no code: 'Create a plan in 3-5 steps, no code yet.'
Read the plan, deliberately correct at least one step, then have it implemented step by step.
selfCheck: Did you actually correct a step in the plan before any code was written?
Is the finished code closer to what you actually wanted than if you'd just said 'build X' right away?
Where would you have needed to step in if you hadn't read the plan?

title: Building Your Own Agents with the Claude Agent SDK
teaser: The Claude Agent SDK gives you Claude Code's agent loop as a library – for your own programs, not just the ready-made CLI.
body:
## What the SDK is
The Claude Agent SDK is a library for Python and TypeScript. It gives you the same agent loop, the same built-in tools, and the same context management that power Claude Code – but programmable inside your own code instead of the terminal.

## The agent loop comes for free
Normally, working with an LLM means building a loop yourself: send a request, check whether the model wants to use a tool, run the tool, send the result back, repeat. The SDK handles this for you: you call `query()` with a prompt, and the response arrives as a stream of messages – reasoning, tool calls, tool results – until the task is done.

## Defining tools
Built-in tools like Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, and WebSearch work immediately, with nothing to implement yourself. `allowed_tools` sets what the agent may use; `permission_mode` controls how much approval it needs. You can also connect custom MCP servers whenever the agent needs to reach an external system.

## SDK or the ready-made CLI?
For interactive development and one-off tasks, the Claude Code CLI is enough. The SDK is built for CI/CD pipelines, custom applications, and production automation – the same capabilities, embedded in your own program instead of the terminal.
bodyDetail:
## A minimal agent, in real code
```
import asyncio
from claude_agent_sdk import query, ClaudeAgentOptions

async def main():
    async for message in query(
        prompt="Find and fix the bug in auth.py",
        options=ClaudeAgentOptions(allowed_tools=["Read", "Edit", "Bash"]),
    ):
        print(message)

asyncio.run(main())
```
Three lines are enough for a working agent: `query()` starts the loop, `prompt` describes the goal, `options` sets the tools and permission mode. The SDK handles orchestration, context management, and retries behind the scenes.

## Permission modes as a control knob
How much the agent can do without asking is controlled by six modes: `default` requires your own approval callback, `acceptEdits` auto-approves file changes, `plan` runs only read-only tools, `dontAsk` denies anything outside the allowed list, `auto` lets a model classifier decide, and `bypassPermissions` runs everything without asking – meant for sandboxes. (as of July 2026)

## What else is built in
Besides tools, the SDK ships hooks (code that runs at fixed points in the lifecycle, e.g. after every tool call), subagents (specialized sub-agents for focused subtasks, invoked through the Agent tool), and sessions (resume or fork a conversation via a session ID).

## SDK vs. Client SDK vs. Managed Agents
The plain Anthropic Client SDK only gives you the raw API – you write the tool loop yourself. The Agent SDK takes over that loop. Managed Agents go further still: Anthropic runs the agent and the sandbox as a hosted REST service, while the Agent SDK runs in your own process, with sessions stored as JSONL files on your own filesystem.

## Prerequisites
Node.js 18+ or Python 3.10+, an Anthropic API key (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`), installed via `npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` or `pip install claude-agent-sdk`. Use is governed by Anthropic's commercial terms of service. On Windows: if PowerShell blocks venv activation with an execution policy error, run Set-ExecutionPolicy -Scope Process RemoteSigned first.
example: In your own code: `query(prompt="Read README.md and summarize the three most important points", options=ClaudeAgentOptions(allowed_tools=["Read"]))` – the agent reads the file itself and replies as a stream of messages.

quiz.question: What does the Claude Agent SDK handle automatically that you'd otherwise have to build yourself against the raw API?
options: Choosing the pricing plan for usage
Training your own LLM model
The tool-execution loop: sending a request, detecting a tool call, running the tool, sending the result back
Writing your entire product's application code
quiz.explain: The SDK gives you the same agent loop as Claude Code out of the box: it detects tool calls, runs built-in tools, and sends results back – you just call query() with a prompt.

exercise.task: Use the Claude Agent SDK (Python or TypeScript) to build a small agent that reads a file with an intentional bug and fixes it.
steps: Install the SDK (pip install claude-agent-sdk or npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk) and set your ANTHROPIC_API_KEY.
Write a small file with an obvious bug (e.g. division by zero) and call query() with allowed_tools=["Read","Edit"].
Follow the message stream and watch the agent read, reason, and edit the file – without you having written the tool loop yourself.
selfCheck: Did you write a tool-call loop yourself anywhere – or did the SDK handle it?
What happens if you set permission_mode to "plan" instead of "acceptEdits"?
When would you use the Claude Code CLI instead of the SDK for the same task?

title: Writing Your Own MCP Server
teaser: An MCP server exposes tools, data, or templates to any MCP-capable client – from Claude Code to Antigravity.
body:
## Three building blocks
An MCP server offers a client like Claude Code or Antigravity three kinds of capability: Tools (functions the model actively calls, e.g. a database query), Resources (passive data like files or API responses that the application supplies), and Prompts (ready-made templates that users deliberately pick).

## The minimal flow
A server runs as its own process and talks to the client over a fixed protocol (typically standard I/O or HTTP). On startup, the client calls `tools/list`, gets back a list of tool definitions with a JSON schema for the parameters, and calls `tools/call` with the right arguments when needed. So your server only has to: register tools with a name, description, and input schema, and return a result when called.

## Official SDKs
The modelcontextprotocol organization maintains official SDKs for Python, TypeScript, Java, Kotlin, C#, Go, PHP, Ruby, Rust, and Swift – you don't have to implement the protocol by hand.

## Connecting to a client
A finished server gets registered in the client's configuration (e.g. `mcpServers` in Claude Code or `mcp_config.json` in Antigravity), usually with a command and arguments to start the server process. After that it shows up in the client's MCP manager, and its tools become available to the model.
bodyDetail:
## A tool defined concretely
A tool is a JSON object with a name, description, and `inputSchema` following the JSON Schema standard:
```
{
  "name": "searchFlights",
  "description": "Search for available flights",
  "inputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "origin": {"type": "string"},
      "destination": {"type": "string"},
      "date": {"type": "string", "format": "date"}
    },
    "required": ["origin", "destination", "date"]
  }
}
```
The model decides for itself when to call this tool – the client can additionally require user approval before execution actually happens.

## Who controls tools, resources, and prompts
The three building blocks differ not just in content but in who's in control: tools are model-controlled (the model decides when to call them), resources are application-controlled (the application decides what to show the model), prompts are user-controlled (users deliberately pick a template, e.g. via a slash command). Resources have a unique URI like `file:///path/file.md` and a MIME type; resource templates like `travel://activities/{city}` allow parameterized queries.

## One crucial rule for STDIO servers
For servers running over standard I/O, never write anything to stdout that isn't part of the JSON-RPC protocol – any extra output corrupts communication with the client. Logging has to go to stderr or a file instead.

## Combining multiple servers
The real payoff appears once several servers work together: a travel server for flights, a weather server for forecasts, a calendar server for appointments. The client reads resources as context first, then calls tools across multiple servers as needed – a task that would otherwise take hours of manual work happens in a few steps. This exact building-block effect is what makes hand-written servers valuable: a single, well-documented server can be plugged into any MCP-capable client, without building a separate integration for every application.
example: A minimal Python server core (using the official SDK): an `@mcp.tool()` decorator registers a function `get_forecast(latitude, longitude)` as a tool – the client discovers it automatically via `tools/list` and calls it when needed.

quiz.question: What's the difference between an MCP "Tool" and an MCP "Resource"?
options: Tools only run locally, Resources only work over the internet
Resources can only ever be invoked manually by users, never by the model
Tools and Resources are technically identical, only the name differs
A Tool is actively called by the model (e.g. to perform an action), a Resource supplies passive data that the application provides as context
quiz.explain: Tools are model-controlled functions with side effects (e.g. triggering a booking), Resources are application-controlled, passive data sources like files or API responses.

sources.title: MCP Docs: Build an MCP server

exercise.task: Use an official MCP SDK (e.g. Python) to build a minimal server with exactly one tool, and connect it to an MCP-capable client.
steps: Install the official SDK for your language and create a server with a single tool (e.g. a function that returns the current date).
Register the server in your client's MCP configuration (e.g. Claude Code or Antigravity) with a start command and arguments.
Open your client's MCP manager (e.g. /mcp), check that the server is connected, and let the model call your tool.
selfCheck: Did the client discover your tool automatically via tools/list, without you registering it manually anywhere else?
What happens if your server process (on STDIO transport) accidentally logs to stdout instead of stderr?
Could you now connect the same server to a different MCP client without rewriting it?

title: What Is Google Antigravity?
teaser: Antigravity is Google's agent platform of a desktop IDE and a terminal CLI (agy) – since 18 June 2026 the successor to the Gemini CLI for individual users.
body:
## One engine, two interfaces
Google Antigravity has two parts sharing the same "Shared Agent Engine": Antigravity 2.0 is a full desktop IDE with a visual multi-panel interface, Antigravity CLI (the `agy` command) is a lightweight terminal program for SSH and keyboard-first work. Settings and permissions sync between both, and conversations can move back and forth between CLI and IDE.

## Successor to the Gemini CLI
On 19 May 2026, Google announced it would retire the Gemini CLI for individual, free-tier users. Since the cutoff date of 18 June 2026, free Google-account access no longer runs through the Gemini CLI but through Antigravity – enterprise customers and paying API users are exempt from the change.

## Plan tiers and G1 credits
Antigravity has three tiers: a free baseline tier with a weekly-refreshed quota, Google AI Pro with a more generous quota refreshed every five hours, and Google AI Ultra with the highest quota. G1 credits are a separate balance that automatically kicks in once your plan quota runs out (can be switched on or off in settings).

## Even Claude on Ultra
All tiers run Google's own Gemini models. Only the Ultra tier adds third-party models on top – including Claude Sonnet and Claude Opus, plus an open GPT-OSS model.
bodyDetail:
## Why the switch happened
Google frames the move by saying Antigravity is a new platform built for multi-agent workflows that carries over "the most critical features" of the Gemini CLI – agent skills, hooks, subagents, and extensions (called "plugins" there) – but explicitly without full 1:1 parity. Anyone coming from the Gemini CLI gets an automatic migration checklist the first time they run `agy` in a project with an existing Gemini configuration; context files like `GEMINI.md` and `AGENTS.md` stay valid unchanged.

## What G1 credits actually are
G1 credits are a personal top-up balance that acts as fallback billing once your regular plan quota for a period runs out. The "Use G1 Credits" setting (`useG1Credits`) determines whether the CLI is allowed to automatically fall back on this balance when quota is exhausted, or stop instead. A dedicated panel (`/credits`) shows the remaining balance.

## Models by plan tier
Per the Antigravity docs (as of July 2026), Gemini 3.1 Pro and Gemini 3.5 Flash run on every tier. Only Google AI Ultra adds Claude Sonnet 4.6 (thinking), Claude Opus 4.6 (thinking), and GPT-OSS-120b – picked from a dropdown that stays locked to the chosen model for the rest of a running conversation. Fixed extra models, like an image generator, aren't selectable; they're simply active automatically.

## No hard quota numbers
Unlike the old Gemini CLI docs, which used to state clear numbers like "1,000 requests per day," Antigravity describes its quotas only qualitatively: "meaningful quota" (baseline), "high, generous quota" (Pro), "highest, most generous quota" (Ultra) – none with an exact request ceiling. According to the docs, how much quota a task consumes depends on how much work the agent actually does, not on the number of messages.
example: Ask a colleague with a Google AI Ultra subscription whether they can switch between Gemini and Claude models in Antigravity – the model-selection dropdown shows exactly which models are unlocked on their plan tier.

quiz.question: Since when has Antigravity been the official successor to the Gemini CLI for individual Google users?
options: Since 18 June 2026, when free Gemini CLI access for individual users was discontinued
Since Google was founded in 1998
Not until 2027 – both tools currently still run in parallel without restriction
Never – the Gemini CLI and Antigravity are two completely unrelated products
quiz.explain: Google announced the switch on 19 May 2026; since the 18 June 2026 cutoff, free individual access runs through Antigravity instead of the Gemini CLI.

sources.title: Google announcement: Transitioning Gemini CLI to Antigravity CLI
Antigravity CLI Docs: Migrating from the Gemini CLI

title: Agent Observability: Seeing What Your Agent Actually Does
teaser: Logs, cost tracking, and OpenTelemetry support turn a black-box session into traceable data – essential once agents run automated or in a team.
body:
## Why watching matters
As long as you work with an agent alone and interactively, you see directly what's happening. Once agents run automated, in a team, or in CI/CD, you need real data instead: what the agent did, what it cost, which tools it called.

## Session logs as the base layer
Claude Code writes every session to disk as a JSONL file (under `~/.claude/projects/`). The format is internal and changes between versions – don't parse these files directly; use `/export` or the documented interfaces instead. Even on subscription plans, `/usage` shows a cost and usage breakdown right in the terminal.

## Cost tracking in the terminal
The `/usage` command shows estimated cost and token usage for the current session right in the terminal, broken down by skills, subagents, plugins, and individual MCP servers. The figure is a local estimate computed from token counts, not an authoritative bill.

## OpenTelemetry for the bigger picture
At team or organization scale, Claude Code offers native OpenTelemetry support: setting `CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY=1` exports metrics, events, and optionally traces to your own observability backend (Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog, and so on). That gives you cost, tool usage, and security events centrally across every user – instead of collecting logs from each machine by hand.
bodyDetail:
## What OpenTelemetry actually is
OpenTelemetry is a vendor-neutral open source framework that standardizes three signal types: traces (request flows through a system), metrics (quantitative measurements), and logs (event text). Tools integrate with OpenTelemetry because it offers one unified protocol (OTLP) instead of a custom integration for every backend.

## What Claude Code actually exports
Once enabled, Claude Code exports metrics including `claude_code.session.count`, `claude_code.cost.usage`, and `claude_code.token.usage`, plus events like `user_prompt`, `tool_result`, `tool_decision`, and `mcp_server_connection`. Every metric carries attributes like session ID, user ID, and model name – so you can break down cost by team, model, or user. Traces (beta) additionally link prompts to the API calls and tool executions that follow from them.

## Fine control: what actually gets logged
By default, metrics and events don't contain raw file contents or prompt text. Specific environment variables open that up deliberately: `OTEL_LOG_USER_PROMPTS=1` for prompt text, `OTEL_LOG_TOOL_DETAILS=1` for tool parameters and file paths, `OTEL_LOG_RAW_API_BODIES=1` for full API request/response bodies. That's a deliberate trade-off between audit depth and privacy.

## Cost in numbers (as of July 2026)
Per the Claude Code docs, average cost across enterprise deployments runs around $13 per active developer-day, or $150–250 per month, with 90% of users staying under $30 per active day. That figure swings widely with model choice, codebase size, and whether multiple agent instances run in parallel (agent teams reportedly use about seven times more tokens than a standard session when teammates run in plan mode, per the docs).

## The limits of observing
Metrics and traces show what the agent did – not automatically whether it was right. For security audits, you still need full session logs (JSONL under `~/.claude/projects/`) and the detail flags turned on, because aggregated metrics can hide individual suspicious tool calls. The JSONL format is internal and changes between versions – `/export` or the documented interfaces are a better fit for access than parsing it yourself.
example: `export CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY=1`, `export OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER=otlp`, and `export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:4317` before starting `claude` are enough to send metrics to a local OpenTelemetry collector setup.

quiz.question: What's the simplest way to enable OpenTelemetry export in Claude Code?
options: It's on by default automatically and can't be turned off
Only through a separate paid add-on subscription with Anthropic
Set the environment variable CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY=1 plus configure exporters and an OTLP endpoint
By running /doctor
quiz.explain: OpenTelemetry export is opt-in: CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY=1 turns it on, and further variables like OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER and OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT determine where the data goes.

sources.title: Claude Code Docs: Manage costs effectively

exercise.task: Turn on local cost and session transparency in Claude Code without setting up an external observability stack.
steps: Run /usage in an active Claude Code session and read the breakdown by skills, subagents, and MCP servers.
Open /usage and look at the cost/usage breakdown.
Set CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY=1 and OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER=console as a test, restart claude, and watch which metrics show up in the console.
selfCheck: Could /usage tell you which part of the session (e.g. a specific MCP server) consumed the most context?
What was in the JSONL line – was it enough for you to reconstruct the cost?
Which of the OTEL_LOG_* variables would you NOT enable in a real team setup because it exports data that's too sensitive?

title: Running Open Models Locally (Ollama & Co.)
teaser: Ollama runs an open language model on your own machine – no cloud, no API key, with clear limits compared to large cloud models.
body:
## What Ollama does
Ollama is an open source program that downloads and runs open language models like Gemma, Qwen, DeepSeek, or gpt-oss locally on your machine. A command like `ollama run gemma4` downloads the model the first time and then starts a chat right in your terminal – no cloud, no API key, no per-request running costs.

## What's realistic
Small models (a few billion parameters) run fine on ordinary laptop hardware using just the CPU, though slower than in the cloud. For bigger models, Ollama automatically uses a GPU if one is available – supporting NVIDIA cards, AMD Radeon via ROCm, Apple Silicon via Metal, and additionally Intel and other AMD cards via Vulkan. If a model doesn't fit entirely in GPU memory, Ollama automatically splits it between GPU and CPU.

## The limits versus cloud models
Local open models are smaller than the largest cloud models and usually fall short on complex reasoning, long context, or specialist knowledge. Memory needs grow with model size and context length; a model that's too big simply won't load, or crawls painfully slowly on CPU.

## When it's worth it
Local models fit privacy-sensitive tasks, offline work, cost-free experimentation, and simple, repetitive jobs. For demanding coding or hard reasoning, cloud models like Claude usually stay ahead.
bodyDetail:
## The command set in practice
`ollama run <model>` downloads (if needed) and starts a chat; `/bye` inside the chat ends the session. Ollama also exposes a REST API on `localhost:11434` (`curl http://localhost:11434/api/chat -d '{...}'`), so local models plug into your own programs like any other API – including official Python and JavaScript libraries. The full model list with sizes and variants lives at ollama.com/library.

## What actually limits hardware
